description: Add a SIGHUP handler to scripts/cron-runtime.ts so schedule edits and added/removed crons/*.md files take effect without restarting the system-cron session (closes the documented schedule hot-reload gap left by #47).

userStories.title: Track live job handles so the prior generation can be stopped
userStories.description: As the cron runtime, I need references to the croner jobs I create so a reschedule can stop them before arming new ones, avoiding duplicate overlapping timers.
acceptanceCriteria: constructCron(entry) returns the live Cron handle it creates instead of void
The mkCron parameter type in scheduleAll is widened to exactly `(entry: CronEntry) => Cron | void` (NOT `=> Cron`) so existing `() => void` test spies still compile; default stays constructCron
scheduleAll() collects truthy handles from each successful mkCron(entry) call into a module-level activeJobs registry, cleared at the START of each call; void/undefined returns are not registered
An exported resetActiveJobs() (or equivalent reset seam) lets tests clear the module-level registry between cases
scheduleAll() still returns BootResult { scheduled, skipped } with unchanged disjoint counting semantics
Typecheck passes
Tests pass

userStories.title: Exported, testable SIGHUP handler reschedules without exiting
userStories.description: As an operator, I want to send SIGHUP to the cron runtime so it re-reads crons/ and re-arms all schedules without restarting the tmux session.
acceptanceCriteria: An exported function (e.g. `export function sighupHandler(): void`) holds the reschedule logic so tests invoke it directly without calling main()
main() registers it via `process.on("SIGHUP", () => setImmediate(sighupHandler))` (deferred out of the signal-callback context)
sighupHandler stops every handle in the activeJobs registry via .stop(), then calls scheduleAll() to re-read and re-arm crons/
sighupHandler does NOT call process.exit() and does NOT remove the PID file
A module-level boolean reload-lock makes the handler re-entrancy-safe: a SIGHUP arriving while a reload is in progress is a no-op; the lock is cleared in a finally block
SIGTERM/SIGINT continue to run the existing cleanup (unlink PID file, exit 0) unchanged
Typecheck passes
Tests pass

userStories.title: Reload is fault-isolated and logs a RELOAD liveness line
userStories.description: As the unattended harness, I want a malformed cron file present during a reload to be isolated (not crash the runtime) and each successful reload recorded.
acceptanceCriteria: A cron file with an invalid schedule present during a SIGHUP reload is dropped by the existing loadCrons() path (logged SCHED_INVALID); other crons remain scheduled and the process does not exit/crash
On a successful reschedule, sighupHandler calls the existing private log() helper directly as `log("system", "RELOAD", "<N> scheduled, <M> skipped")` (no inline appendFileSync, no duplicated format)
id is "system" matching the BOOT precedent
Typecheck passes
Tests pass

userStories.title: Test coverage for the SIGHUP reload path
userStories.description: As a maintainer, I want the reschedule behavior covered by the existing test suite so a refactor can't silently regress it.
acceptanceCriteria: A new describe("SIGHUP reload") block in scripts/__tests__/cron-runtime.test.ts calls the exported sighupHandler directly (not scheduleAll), with resetActiveJobs() between cases
A test asserts prior job handles have .stop() called on reschedule (mkCron returning fake handles with a .stop spy)
A test asserts a newly-added cron file in the tmp crons dir is picked up, and a removed file is dropped, after invoking the handler against updated directory state
A test asserts a RELOAD line is written via the existing appendFileSync spy on reload
A test asserts a reload with an empty prior activeJobs registry does not throw
A test asserts the re-entrancy lock: a second invocation while a reload is in progress is a safe no-op (does not double-register)
No test touches the real crons/ directory (uses the existing beforeEach/afterEach tmp dir pattern); no new external dependencies
vitest run (root test:scripts) passes with zero failures
Typecheck passes
Tests pass

userStories.title: Document the SIGHUP reload path
userStories.description: As an operator reading the docs, I want to know how to reload cron schedules without a restart, including from the host where the runtime runs inside the container.
acceptanceCriteria: crons/README.md documents the reload command as `docker exec -u sandbox openharness kill -HUP "$(cat crons/.pid)"`, noting the bare host-side form only works from inside the container (PID namespace), and uses the correct PID path crons/.pid (not .cron.lock)
The note includes a pre-send health check: `docker exec -u sandbox openharness sh -c 'kill -0 "$(cat crons/.pid)" 2>/dev/null && echo alive || echo "not running"'`
The note includes an escape hatch: if a reload arms zero crons, restart the runtime (tmux kill-session -t system-cron then the documented start) to restore the last good state
A new RELOAD row is added to the status-tokens table in crons/README.md (id is system; msg is the cron count)

The stale Hot-reload sentence ('Frontmatter changes ... require a runtime restart; no watcher is implemented.') is corrected to say schedule/frontmatter changes and added/removed cron files now take effect via a SIGHUP reload (still no auto-watcher); a full restart is only for runtime code changes
A CHANGELOG.md [Unreleased] entry under ### Added records the SIGHUP cron-reload capability, linking issue #88
Typecheck passes
